does: This paper systematically catalogs and analyzes thirteen publicly released prompt corpora used to evaluate refusal behavior in coding language models, including AdvBench, CyberSecEval, RMCBench, RedCode, and others released between 2023-2025. Following a PRISMA-style protocol, the authors extract and standardize information about each corpus along dimensions including construction methodology, prompt taxonomy (modality, turn structure, elicitation style), reproducibility standards, and licensing terms. Rather than treating code security or jailbreak techniques as primary, the paper treats the benchmark datasets themselves as the central object of study, providing a unified framework for understanding how these diverse evaluation resources differ in their design choices, validation rigor, and practical applicability to assessing model safety on malicious-coding tasks.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in AI safety evaluation and jailbreak research that emerged as language models became capable of generating executable code. It responds to the fragmentation problem in benchmark design: while prior work surveyed code security vulnerabilities, jailbreak taxonomies, or vulnerability detection methods in passing, these earlier efforts lacked a unified perspective on the evaluation infrastructure itself. The paper extends systematic review methodologies (PRISMA) from medical research into machine learning evaluation, joining recent efforts to standardize and compare benchmark protocols. It connects to earlier work on refusal evaluation and model alignment that highlighted the need for reproducible, well-documented test sets, while addressing the specific challenge that coding-LLM safety evaluation has splintered across many incompatible corpora with varying construction standards.
enables: This systematic comparison provides a foundation for consolidating fragmented evaluation efforts in coding-LLM safety. By standardizing metadata extraction and highlighting methodological differences, the work enables researchers to select appropriate corpora for their evaluation needs, understand compatibility and reusability across benchmarks, and identify gaps in existing coverage. It sets up future work toward a unified or harmonized evaluation framework that could improve inter-study comparability and reduce redundant corpus construction. The catalogue also supports the development of best practices for prompt-corpus design, informs decisions about which refusal evaluation methods transfer across benchmarks, and points toward meta-analyses of model performance that aggregate across existing corpora despite their heterogeneity. This work potentially enables more principled design of future coding-safety benchmarks grounded in what prior corpora have learned.
